segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a blue title screen displaying 'C Cube Academy' and an Instagram call-to-action, accompanied by light, whimsical background music. This transitions to a starry night sky, then to a calm rooftop setting with a wooden floor and white railing, overlooking a dark cityscape. A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt, dark pants, and light sneakers, is seen using green binoculars to observe the sky. Another boy, with short brown hair, glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers, approaches him. With a clear, youthful voice, the boy in yellow asks in French, "Qu'est-ce que tu fais avec ces jumelles au milieu de la nuit?" (What are you doing with these binoculars in the middle of the night?), punctuated by a light sound effect. The boy with the binoculars, also speaking with a clear, youthful voice, confidently replies in French, "J'essaie d'identifier les planètes du système solaire" (I'm trying to identify the planets of the solar system), explaining that he has an oral exam on the solar system tomorrow. The boy in yellow, adopting a tutoring role, acknowledges this and then asks, "Dis-moi, tout d'abord, c'est quoi le système solaire?" (Tell me, first of all, what is the solar system?). The boy with the binoculars confidently defines it in French as "l'ensemble de soleil et les astres qui gravitent autour de lui, y compris notre planète" (the sun and all the celestial bodies that orbit around it, including our planet), as the background music continues to create an educational atmosphere.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a blue title screen displaying 'C Cube Academy' and an Instagram call-to-action, transitioning into a starry night sky. The scene shifts to a rooftop at night where a young boy in a blue checkered shirt and dark pants is using binoculars to look at the sky. Another boy, wearing a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and glasses, approaches him. The boy in yellow questions him in French, asking what he's doing with the binoculars at night, with subtitles providing the English translation. The boy with the binoculars explains he's trying to identify planets in the solar system for an upcoming oral exam. The boy in yellow, now acting as a tutor, asks him to define the solar system. The boy responds confidently, describing it as the sun and the celestial bodies orbiting it, including planets like Earth. The boy in yellow praises his answer and asks how many planets are in the solar system, setting up the next part of the lesson. The rooftop setting features a wooden floor and a white railing, with a dark cityscape and stars visible in the background, creating a calm, educational atmosphere.

audio_updated_entities.Boy with Binoculars: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers, holding green binoculars. He is initially focused on the sky, then turns to answer questions with a confident expression, speaking French with a clear, youthful voice to explain he's identifying planets for an oral exam and defining the solar system.
audio_updated_entities.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He approaches the other boy, gestures while speaking, and acts as a tutor, asking questions about the solar system in French with a clear, youthful, and inquisitive voice.
audio_updated_entities.Rooftop Setting: A wooden rooftop with a white railing, overlooking a dark cityscape under a starry night sky, serving as the backdrop for the educational interaction, enhanced by light, whimsical background music.

segments.integrated_caption: On a nighttime rooftop overlooking a dark cityscape under a starry sky, a young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt and holding green binoculars (Boy with Binoculars), stands beside a boy with short brown hair and glasses (Boy in Yellow Shirt). With light, inquisitive background music playing, the Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in French, asks, "Très bien. Et combien de planètes y a-t-il dans le système solaire?" (Very good. And how many planets are there in the solar system?). The Boy with Binoculars confidently replies, "Il y en a 8" (There are 8), followed by a positive chime sound. The scene then transitions to a starry space background displaying the eight planets, each labeled with its French name, which the Boy with Binoculars recites: "Mercure, Vénus, Terre, Mars, Jupiter, Saturne, Uranus, et Neptune." Another chime sounds as he finishes. Returning to the rooftop, the Boy in Yellow Shirt, with an encouraging tone, asks, "Excellent. Est-ce que tu connais les caractéristiques de chacune de ces planètes?" (Excellent. Do you know the characteristics of each of these planets?). The Boy with Binoculars admits with uncertainty, "Non, je ne sais pas" (No, I don't know). The Boy in Yellow Shirt reassures him, "Ce n'est pas grave" (It's not serious), and then, with a magical chime sound, excitedly invites him, "Veux-tu voyager virtuellement avec moi et visiter chacune d'elles?" (Do you want to travel virtually with me and visit each of them?). The Boy with Binoculars enthusiastically agrees, "Oui, bien sûr. Ça m'intéresse beaucoup!" (Yes, of course. That interests me a lot!). As he speaks, the Boy in Yellow Shirt gestures, and a white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone suddenly appears on the rooftop with a distinct "whoosh" sound. The rocket then launches with a fiery blast and a loud engine sound, initiating their virtual space adventure, all while the background music continues.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens on a rooftop at night, where the Boy with Binoculars stands beside the Boy in Yellow Shirt, who gestures while asking about the number of planets in the solar system. The Boy with Binoculars confidently answers 'There are 8,' and the scene transitions to a starry space background displaying the eight planets in order, each labeled with its French name: Mercure, Vénus, Terre, Mars, Jupiter, Saturne, Uranus, and Neptune. Returning to the rooftop, the Boy in Yellow Shirt asks if the other boy knows the characteristics of each planet, to which the Boy with Binoculars admits he does not. The Boy in Yellow Shirt reassures him and invites him on a virtual journey to visit each planet. The Boy with Binoculars agrees enthusiastically, and the Boy in Yellow Shirt gestures toward a rocket that suddenly appears, launching with a fiery blast. He instructs the boy to get on the rocket, initiating their virtual space adventure. The rooftop setting remains consistent throughout, with a dark cityscape and starry sky, while the dialogue is supported by bilingual French-English subtitles.

He begins with a confident expression, answers questions, shows uncertainty when asked about planetary characteristics, and then becomes excited at the prospect of a virtual journey.

Ça m'intéresse beaucoup!' (Yes, of course. That interests me a lot!) to the virtual journey.
audio_updated_entities.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as a tutor, speaking in French with a young boy's voice, asking questions like 'combien de planètes y a-t-il dans le système solaire?' and 'Est-ce que tu connais les caractéristiques de chacune de ces planètes?', and making encouraging remarks such as 'Très bien', 'Excellent', 'Ce n'est pas grave', and 'Parfait'. He invites the other boy on a virtual journey, remaining calm and friendly throughout the interaction.
audio_updated_entities.Rocket: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and yellow flames, appearing suddenly on the rooftop with a distinct 'whoosh' sound and launching with a fiery blast and loud engine sound, serving as the vehicle for the virtual journey.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens on a rooftop at night, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt gestures excitedly toward a suddenly appearing Rocket, accompanied by a distinct whirring sound. He invites the Boy with Binoculars to board, saying in French, "Monte dans cette fusée." The Boy with Binoculars, initially holding his green binoculars, expresses awe at the rocket, exclaiming in French, "Waouh, une fusée ! C'est génial !" The Rocket then launches with a powerful whoosh and a burst of yellow flames, carrying both boys into the starry expanse of space, as gentle, adventurous background music begins. As they soar through the cosmos, the Boy in Yellow Shirt explains in French that their journey begins with the first planet of the solar system, Mercury, stating, "On commence par la toute première planète de notre système solaire, qui est Mercure." The Rocket approaches a large, cratered gray planet—Mercury—set against a vibrant nebula backdrop, accompanied by another whoosh sound. The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues to narrate in French, stating, "C'est la planète la plus proche du soleil et la plus petite," and that its temperature fluctuates extremely, "Sa température varie entre -173 et 427 degrés Celsius," displayed via a red thermometer graphic. He adds that a year on Mercury lasts 88 days, shown with a calendar icon. The Boy with Binoculars reacts to this information, saying in French, "C'est très instable," to which the Boy in Yellow Shirt replies, "Oui." Throughout, the boys remain seated on the Rocket, smiling and engaged, as the environment shifts from the urban rooftop to the vast, twinkling void of space, all underscored by the continuous adventurous music.
segments.visual_original_caption: The scene opens on a rooftop at night, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt gestures excitedly toward a suddenly appearing Rocket, inviting the Boy with Binoculars to board. The Boy with Binoculars, initially holding his green binoculars, expresses awe at the rocket, exclaiming its awesomeness. The Rocket then launches with a burst of yellow flames, carrying both boys into the starry expanse of space. As they soar through the cosmos, the Boy in Yellow Shirt explains their journey begins with the first planet of the solar system, Mercury. The Rocket approaches a large, cratered gray planet—Mercury—set against a vibrant nebula backdrop. The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues to narrate, stating Mercury is the closest and smallest planet to the Sun, with extreme temperature fluctuations between -173°C and 427°C, displayed via a red thermometer graphic. He adds that a year on Mercury lasts 88 days, shown with a calendar icon. Throughout, the boys remain seated on the Rocket, smiling and engaged, as the environment shifts from the urban rooftop to the vast, twinkling void of space.

audio_updated_entities.Mercury: A large, gray, cratered planet depicted in space, shown against a colorful nebula. It is identified as the first planet of the solar system, with on-screen text and graphics highlighting its proximity to the Sun, small size, extreme temperature range (-173°C to 427°C), and 88-day year, all narrated by the Boy in Yellow Shirt.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on their white and gray Rocket, floating in space near Mercury, a large, gray, cratered planet against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, with a calm and educational tone, explains in French, "Et là-bas, une année dure 88 jours" (And there, a year lasts 88 days), referring to Mercury's 88-day year. The Boy with Binoculars, with an amazed voice, exclaims in French, "C'est incroyable!" (That's incredible!). The Rocket then propels forward with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Mercury behind as the boys transition to their next destination. The environment shifts dramatically to a fiery, orange-hued space dominated by the massive, bright Sun, with Venus now appearing as a large, glowing, rocky planet. The Boy in Yellow Shirt announces in French, "Passons à notre deuxième planète, qui est Vénus" (Let's move to our second planet, which is Venus), and informs the Boy with Binoculars that Venus is the second closest planet to the Sun, adding, "Elle est appelée planète jumelle de la Terre" (It is called Earth's twin planet) due to their similar size and composition. The Boy with Binoculars, with a surprised and curious tone, asks in French, "C'est vrai? Je ne le savais pas. Et pourquoi?" (Is that true? I didn't know that. And why?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt calmly replies in French, "Tout simplement parce qu'elles sont semblables, de point de vue dimension et composition" (Simply because they are similar, from the point of view of size and composition). The Boy with Binoculars then inquires in French, "Donc, Vénus est habitable?" (So, is Venus habitable?), to which the Boy in Yellow Shirt responds in French, "Autrefois, peut-être qu'elle l'était" (In the past, perhaps it was), hinting at its potential for past habitability. Throughout, the Rocket continues to carry them smoothly through space, with the Sun’s intense glow illuminating the scene.
segments.visual_original_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the Rocket, floating in space near Mercury, which is depicted as a large, gray, cratered planet against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that a year on Mercury lasts 88 days, prompting the Boy with Binoculars to exclaim in amazement. The Rocket then propels forward, leaving Mercury behind as the boys transition to their next destination. The environment shifts dramatically to a fiery, orange-hued space dominated by the Sun, with Venus now appearing as a large, glowing, rocky planet. The Boy in Yellow Shirt informs the Boy with Binoculars that Venus is the second closest planet to the Sun and is often called Earth’s twin due to their similar size and composition. The Boy with Binoculars expresses surprise and curiosity, asking why, to which the Boy in Yellow Shirt replies that it’s because of their similarities in size and composition. The Boy with Binoculars then inquires if Venus is habitable, and the Boy in Yellow Shirt responds that scientists once believed it might have had liquid water on its surface, hinting at its potential for past habitability. Throughout, the Rocket continues to carry them smoothly through space, with the Sun’s intense glow illuminating the scene.

audio_updated_entities.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, gesturing and speaking confidently in a calm, educational French voice as he explains Mercury’s characteristics and then transitions to Venus, providing scientific facts.
audio_updated_entities.Rocket: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and yellow flames, carrying the two boys through space. It moves smoothly from Mercury to Venus, serving as their vehicle for exploration, with the boys seated on top throughout the journey, and produces a distinct whooshing sound during propulsion.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the Rocket, now flying near the Sun, with Venus prominently displayed as a large, fiery orange planet in the foreground. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in a calm French voice, explains that scientists believe Venus once had liquid water on its surface, adding "Mais maintenant non" (But now no), a point emphasized by a subtle chime sound. The Boy with Binoculars, with a curious French voice, then asks about Venus’s orbital period, "De combien de temps cette planète a besoin pour faire une rotation autour du soleil?" (How much time does this planet need to make a rotation around the sun?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt answers, revealing it takes about 225 Earth days, accompanied by another chime. The Boy with Binoculars, still curious, asks, "Et quelle est sa température?" (And what is its temperature?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues, stating Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system, with a temperature of 460°C, which is visually emphasized by a digital thermometer graphic and a chime sound. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with shock, exclaiming "460 degrés Celsius? Oh mon Dieu!" (460 degrees Celsius? Oh my God!) in a surprised tone, followed by another chime. The Boy in Yellow Shirt then announces, "Et maintenant, c'est le tour de notre planète Terre" (And now, it's the turn of our planet Earth), signaling they are moving on to Earth, with the Boy with Binoculars excitedly responding, "Oui, on y va!" (Yes, let's go!). The Rocket accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Venus and the Sun behind as the scene transitions to a dark, star-filled space with Earth appearing in the distance, glowing with blue oceans and white clouds, accompanied by the sustained hum of the rocket engine, signaling the next destination in their journey.
segments.visual_original_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the Rocket, now flying near the Sun, with Venus prominently displayed as a large, fiery orange planet in the foreground. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that scientists believe Venus once had liquid water on its surface, and then answers the Boy with Binoculars’ question about Venus’s orbital period, revealing it takes about 225 Earth days. He continues by stating Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system, with a temperature of 460°C, which is visually emphasized by a digital thermometer graphic. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with shock, exclaiming 'Oh my God!' at the extreme heat. The Boy in Yellow Shirt then announces they are moving on to Earth, and the Rocket accelerates, leaving Venus and the Sun behind as the scene transitions to a dark, star-filled space with Earth appearing in the distance, glowing with blue oceans and white clouds, signaling the next destination in their journey.

He is seated on the Rocket, initially surprised by Venus’s 225-day year, then visibly shocked at its 460°C temperature, and finally excited as they transition to Earth.
active_entities.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, calmly explaining Venus’s characteristics, gesturing as he speaks, and then announcing the transition to Earth with a confident tone.
active_entities.Rocket: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and yellow flames, carrying the two boys through space. It moves steadily near the Sun and Venus, then accelerates as they leave Venus behind, heading toward Earth.
active_entities.Venus: A large, glowing, rocky planet with a fiery orange and yellow surface, shown in close proximity to the Sun. It is described as Earth’s twin and highlighted for its extreme heat (460°C) and 225-day orbit.
active_entities.Sun: A massive, bright, orange-yellow star with visible solar flares and a glowing corona, dominating the background as the Rocket approaches Venus. It provides intense illumination and a warm, radiant atmosphere for the scene.

audio_updated_entities.Boy with Binoculars: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers. He is seated on the Rocket, initially surprised by Venus’s 225-day year, then visibly shocked at its 460°C temperature, exclaiming "460 degrés Celsius? Oh mon Dieu!" in a surprised French voice, and finally excited as they transition to Earth, saying "Oui, on y va!" in an eager French voice.
audio_updated_entities.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, calmly explaining Venus’s characteristics in a clear French voice, stating "En fait, les scientifiques pensent qu'il y avait de l'eau liquide à sa surface. Mais maintenant non," and later announcing the transition to Earth with a confident tone, "Et maintenant, c'est le tour de notre planète Terre."
audio_updated_entities.Rocket: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and yellow flames, carrying the two boys through space. It moves steadily near the Sun and Venus, then accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound and a sustained engine hum as they leave Venus behind, heading toward Earth.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Rocket, making whooshing sounds, carrying the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars through space as they approach Earth, a large, blue and white planet. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in a clear, instructional male voice, explains that "Notre Terre est à la troisième position" (Our Earth is in the third position), followed by a ding sound. He continues, stating that "365 jours sont nécessaires à la Terre pour tourner autour du soleil" (365 days are necessary for Earth to turn around the sun), with a '365 jours' sign appearing on screen. The Boy with Binoculars, in a slightly higher-pitched, curious male voice, responds, "Oui, tu as raison. Je le sais." (Yes, you are right. I know it.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt then details the seasons, explaining that "Quand l'hémisphère nord est du côté du soleil, c'est l'été dans le nord et l'hiver dans le sud. Et vice-versa." (When the northern hemisphere is on the side of the sun, it's summer in the north and winter in the south. And vice-versa.), punctuated by another ding. The Boy with Binoculars finds this "intéressant" (interesting) and, showing excitement, asks, "Parle-moi de Mars." (Tell me about Mars.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt agrees, "Allons à Mars." (Let's go to Mars.), and with a whoosh and accelerating engine sounds, the Rocket rapidly accelerates, leaving Earth behind amidst high-pitched whirring and dinging. The scene transitions to Mars, a large, reddish-brown planet against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, still guiding, states, "C'est la quatrième planète de notre système solaire." (It's the fourth planet of our solar system.), followed by a ding, as a '687 jours' sign appears, indicating Mars's year length. The Rocket continues its journey toward Mars through the star-filled expanse, with the boys engaged in conversation.
segments.visual_original_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars riding the Rocket through space, now approaching Earth, which is shown as a large, blue and white planet with visible continents and cloud cover. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that Earth is in the third position in the solar system, and that it takes 365 days for Earth to revolve around the Sun, with a '365 jours' sign appearing on screen. He then explains the seasons, stating that when the northern hemisphere is tilted toward the Sun, it is summer in the North and winter in the South, and vice versa. The Boy with Binoculars responds with interest, asking to learn about Mars. The Boy in Yellow Shirt agrees, and they accelerate the Rocket, leaving Earth behind. The scene transitions to Mars, a large, reddish-brown planet set against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt points out that Mars is the fourth planet in the solar system, and a sign appears indicating that a year on Mars lasts 687 days. The Rocket continues its journey toward Mars, with the two boys engaged in conversation as they travel through the star-filled expanse.

active_entities.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, calmly explaining Earth’s position and orbit, gesturing as he speaks, and then announcing the transition to Mars with a confident tone.
active_entities.Boy with Binoculars: A young boy with spiky black hair, wearing a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light-colored sneakers. He is seated on the Rocket, initially attentive as Earth is explained, then shows curiosity and excitement when asked about Mars, and finally looks forward as they head toward Mars.

active_entities.Earth: A large, blue and white planet with visible continents, oceans, and cloud cover, shown in the distance against a dark, star-filled space. It appears as the next destination, glowing softly as the Rocket approaches and then recedes into the background.

Je le sais."), expressing interest ("C'est intéressant."), and asking to learn about Mars ("Parle-moi de Mars."), finally looking forward as they head toward Mars.

It appears as the next destination, glowing softly as the Rocket approaches and then recedes into the background, and is the subject of the Boy in Yellow Shirt's explanation.
audio_updated_entities.Mars: A large, reddish-brown planet with visible surface features, set against a colorful nebula. It is introduced as the fourth planet in the solar system, with a '687 jours' sign appearing to indicate the length of its year, and is the subject of the Boy in Yellow Shirt's explanation and the destination.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the Rocket, now approaching Mars, a large, reddish-brown planet against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking in clear French, gestures toward Mars and explains that its year lasts 687 days, a fact confirmed by on-screen text and punctuated by a distinct chime sound. He continues, stating that Mars' temperature varies between -140°C and 30°C, accompanied by a digital thermometer graphic and another chime. He then explains that Mars is called 'the Red Planet,' prompting the Boy with Binoculars, also speaking in French, to curiously ask, "D'où vient ce nom?" (Where does this name come from?). The Boy in Yellow Shirt responds that its soil is composed mainly of red iron oxide, hence the name, with a final chime. With a whooshing sound and accelerating rocket engine noises, the Boy in Yellow Shirt announces it’s time to head for Jupiter, and the Rocket begins to move away from Mars. The background shifts from the colorful nebula to dark, star-filled space as the Rocket, with more intense engine sounds, approaches Jupiter, a massive gas giant with distinct brown and white bands. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, with a chime, informs the viewer that Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system and is composed mostly of hydrogen and helium. He then asks about Jupiter’s revolution around the Sun, setting the stage for the next segment of their journey, all against a backdrop of subtle space-themed ambient sounds.

He then points out that Mars' temperature varies between -140°C and 30°C, accompanied by a digital thermometer graphic. He explains that Mars is called 'the Red Planet' due to its soil being composed mainly of red iron oxide, which the Boy with Binoculars listens to with growing curiosity. After this explanation, the Boy in Yellow Shirt announces it’s time to head for Jupiter, and the Rocket begins to move away from Mars. segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the white and gray Rocket, now floating in space near Jupiter, a massive gas giant with swirling brown and white bands. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, speaking calmly, explains that although Jupiter’s days are the shortest, its years are very long, taking about 12 Earth years to revolve around the Sun, a fact visually reinforced by a floating '12 années' sign. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with a surprised, audible exclamation of "Oh, c'est trop long" (Oh, that's too long), to which the Boy in Yellow Shirt agrees, "Oui, c'est trop long" (Yes, that's too long). The Boy in Yellow Shirt then suggests, "Maintenant, allons à Saturne" (Now, let's go to Saturn), and the Rocket accelerates with distinct whooshing and engine sounds, its yellow flames becoming more pronounced as it leaves Jupiter behind. The scene transitions to Saturn, a pale yellow planet with prominent, thick rings. The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues his explanation, stating that Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and, audibly describing it as "la plus grande planète du système solaire" (the largest planet in the solar system) – a detail that contrasts with its visual description as the second largest – and famous for its beautiful thick ringlets. The Boy with Binoculars then asks, "En combien de temps elle orbite autour du soleil?" (How long does it orbit around the sun?), and the Boy in Yellow Shirt responds that Saturn needs "29,5 années terrestres" (29.5 Earth years) to orbit the Sun, as a '29,5 années' sign appears above. The Rocket remains near Saturn, with the vast, dark space and distant stars forming the backdrop.
segments.visual_original_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on the Rocket, now floating in space near Jupiter, a massive gas giant with swirling brown and white bands. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that although Jupiter’s days are the shortest, its years are very long, taking about 12 Earth years to revolve around the Sun, as indicated by a floating '12 années' sign. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with surprise, and the Boy in Yellow Shirt suggests moving on to Saturn. The Rocket then accelerates, leaving Jupiter behind, and transitions to Saturn, a pale yellow planet with prominent, thick rings. The Boy in Yellow Shirt informs the viewer that Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second largest in the solar system, famous for its beautiful ringlets. He then asks how long it takes Saturn to orbit the Sun, and the answer appears: '29,5 années', with a corresponding sign floating above. The Rocket remains near Saturn as the explanation concludes, with the vast, dark space and distant stars forming the backdrop.

He later looks forward with curiosity as they approach Saturn, and asks "En combien de temps elle orbite autour du soleil?" (How long does it orbit around the sun?) in a curious tone.
audio_updated_entities.Boy in Yellow Shirt: A boy with short brown hair, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown cargo pants, and dark sneakers. He acts as the guide, calmly explaining Jupiter’s orbit and year, gesturing as he speaks, and audibly stating its revolution takes "12 années terrestres environ" (about 12 Earth years). He acknowledges the other boy's surprise with "Oui, c'est trop long" and confidently announces the transition to Saturn with a forward-pointing gesture, saying "Maintenant, allons à Saturne." He continues to explain Saturn’s position, audibly claiming it is "la plus grande planète du système solaire" (the largest planet in the solar system) and famous for its "magnifiques anneaux épais" (magnificent thick rings), finally stating its orbit takes "29,5 années terrestres" (29.5 Earth years).
audio_updated_entities.Rocket: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and yellow flames, carrying the two boys through space. It moves steadily near Jupiter, then accelerates with distinct whooshing and engine sounds as they leave Jupiter behind, heading toward Saturn, with the flames becoming more pronounced during the transition.

segments.integrated_caption: The space journey continues with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars riding the Rocket through the cosmos, accompanied by whimsical background music. As they approach Saturn, a pale yellow gas giant with prominent, multi-layered rings, the Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that it takes 29.5 Earth years to orbit the Sun, a fact emphasized by a floating '29,5 années' sign. The Boy with Binoculars, in a curious, higher-pitched voice, exclaims "C'est très long!" and then asks, "Et quelle est sa température?" The Boy in Yellow Shirt, with a calm, explanatory tone, responds that it varies between -189°C and -139°C. The Boy with Binoculars visibly shivers, audibly stating, "Il gèle là-bas!" The Boy in Yellow Shirt then announces, "Passons à Uranus," and the Rocket accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Saturn's rings in the distance. They approach Uranus, a large, pale blue ice giant set against a deep blue nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains, "Elle est considérée comme une géante de glace." The Boy with Binoculars, still curious, asks, "Et pourquoi?" The Boy in Yellow Shirt clarifies that its main components are water, ammonia, and methane in solid form. The Boy with Binoculars then inquires, "Et son temps de rotation?" (referring to its orbital period). The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that Uranus takes "84 ans" to orbit the Sun, a fact highlighted by a floating '84 ans' sign, adding that "C'est l'espérance de vie moyenne" (the average human life expectancy), as the Boy with Binoculars listens attentively. Throughout the segment, subtle 'pop' or 'ding' sound effects accompany the appearance of signs or transitions.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video continues the space journey with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars riding the Rocket through the cosmos. They approach Saturn, a pale yellow gas giant with prominent, multi-layered rings, set against a dark, star-filled space. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that Saturn takes 29.5 Earth years to orbit the Sun, with a floating '29,5 années' sign appearing above the Rocket to emphasize this. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with surprise, asking about Saturn’s temperature, to which the guide responds that it varies between -189°C and -139°C, causing the boy to shiver in reaction. The scene then transitions as they leave Saturn behind, with the Boy in Yellow Shirt announcing their next destination: Uranus. The Rocket accelerates, leaving Saturn’s rings in the distance, and approaches a large, pale blue planet—Uranus—set against a deep blue nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that Uranus is considered an ice giant, composed of water, ammonia, and methane in solid form, and that it takes 84 years to orbit the Sun, a fact highlighted by a floating '84 ans' sign. He adds that this is the average human life expectancy on Earth, drawing a relatable comparison. The Boy with Binoculars listens attentively, showing curiosity and engagement throughout the educational journey.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars, both speaking French, riding their white and gray Rocket through space, accompanied by a soft whirring sound. They approach Uranus, a large pale blue planet, where the Boy in Yellow Shirt asks, "Et quelle est sa température?" (And what is its temperature?), then explains that Uranus is an ice giant with a temperature varying between -226°C and -197°C, visually confirmed by a digital thermometer graphic. A "boing" sound punctuates the Boy with Binoculars' surprised reaction, exclaiming, "Oh, il fait trop froid là-bas!" (Oh, it's too cold there!). Another "boing" sound is heard as the Boy in Yellow Shirt confirms, "Oui," and announces, "Passons à notre dernière planète, Neptune." (Yes. Let's move on to our last planet, Neptune.) The Rocket then accelerates with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving Uranus behind. The scene transitions to Neptune, a deep blue planet set against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt continues his explanation, stating, "Elle est aussi considérée comme une géante de glace." (It is also considered an ice giant.) Another "boing" sound is heard. He further elaborates that since Neptune isn't entirely solid, "ses parties pourraient orbiter à des vitesses différentes." (its parts could orbit at different speeds.) A "boing" sound precedes the Boy with Binoculars' reaction, "Waouh, c'est étrange." (Wow, that's strange.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt then provides Neptune’s temperature range, "Sa température varie entre -218 et -200 degrés Celsius." (Its temperature varies between -218 and -200 degrees Celsius.), as a new digital thermometer graphic appears. Another "boing" sound is heard before the Boy with Binoculars expresses gratitude, "Merci beaucoup pour toutes ces informations intéressantes." (Thank you very much for all this interesting information.) The Boy in Yellow Shirt calmly responds, "Pas de problème." (No problem.) The Rocket continues its journey through the starry expanse, with the two boys seated calmly, observing the distant planets, accompanied by the ongoing whirring sound.
segments.visual_original_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars riding the Rocket through space, now approaching Uranus, a large pale blue planet. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that Uranus is an ice giant, and its temperature varies between -226°C and -197°C, as shown by a digital thermometer graphic appearing next to the planet. The Boy with Binoculars reacts with surprise, exclaiming it's too cold. The Rocket then accelerates, leaving Uranus behind as the Boy in Yellow Shirt announces their next destination: Neptune. The scene transitions to Neptune, a deep blue planet set against a vibrant nebula. The Boy in Yellow Shirt explains that Neptune is also an ice giant and that its parts could orbit at different speeds, which the Boy with Binoculars finds weird. A new digital thermometer graphic appears, showing Neptune’s temperature range of -218°C to -200°C. The Boy with Binoculars expresses gratitude for the information, and the Boy in Yellow Shirt responds with a simple 'No problem.' The Rocket continues its journey through the starry expanse, with the two boys seated calmly, observing the distant planets.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on a white and gray Rocket, flying through a star-filled space with a large blue planet visible in the background, accompanied by cheerful background music. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, wearing glasses and a yellow polo, confidently speaks in French, gesturing as he says, "Il n'y a pas de quoi!" (No problem!) and then asks, "Maintenant je vais te poser une question, es-tu prêt?" (Now I'm gonna ask you a question, are you ready?). The Boy with Binoculars, in a blue checkered shirt, listens attentively and responds with a simple "Oui." A 'ding' sound effect plays as the Boy in Yellow Shirt then poses the question, "Quelle planète est surnommée « La planète Rouge ?" (Which planet is nicknamed "The Red Planet"?). The Boy with Binoculars, smiling proudly, confidently answers in French, "Oui, je le sais! C'est la planète 'Mars'." (Yes, I know! It is the planet 'Mars'.), followed by another 'ding' sound. The Boy in Yellow Shirt praises him, stating, "Très bien. Tu as tout à fait raison." (Very well. You're absolutely right.), with a final 'ding'. On-screen text appears in both French and English, mirroring the dialogue. The Rocket continues to emit bright yellow flames as it moves forward. The Boy in Yellow Shirt then transitions to a call to action, encouraging viewers in French to "Si vous avez aimé cette vidéo, appuyez sur le bouton « J'aime »." (If you liked this video, hit the "Like" button.) and "Et pour de nouvelles vidéos intéressantes, n'oubliez surtout pas de vous abonner à notre chaîne!" (And for new interesting videos, don't forget to subscribe to our channel!). The Boy with Binoculars joins in, adding, "Merci de votre soutien! À la prochaine!" (Thank you for your support! See you next time!). The Rocket continues its journey through the dark, starry expanse, with the boys smiling and waving as the scene fades out, while the CUBE ACADEMY Logo remains visible in the top right corner.
segments.visual_original_caption: The scene opens with the Boy in Yellow Shirt and the Boy with Binoculars seated on a white and gray Rocket, flying through a star-filled space with a large blue planet visible in the background. The Boy in Yellow Shirt, wearing glasses, a yellow polo shirt, brown pants, and dark sneakers, speaks confidently, gesturing with his hand as he asks the Boy with Binoculars a question. The Boy with Binoculars, in a blue checkered shirt over a white t-shirt, dark pants, and light sneakers, listens attentively, then responds with a smile, correctly identifying Mars as 'The Red Planet.' The Boy in Yellow Shirt praises him, and the two continue their journey through space. The Rocket emits bright yellow flames as it moves forward. On-screen text appears in both French and English, including phrases like 'Il n'y a pas de quoi! No problem!', 'Maintenant je vais te poser une question, es-tu prêt? Now I'm gonna ask you a question, are you ready?', 'Quelle planète est surnommée « La planète Rouge ? Which planet is nicknamed "The Red Planet"?', 'Oui, je le sais! C'est la planète "Mars". Yes, I know! It is the planet "Mars".', and 'Très bien. Tu as tout à fait raison. Very well. You're absolutely right.' The video concludes with a call to action: 'Si vous avez aimé cette vidéo, appuyez sur le bouton « J'aime ». If you liked this video, hit the "Like" button.' followed by 'Et pour de nouvelles vidéos intéressantes, n'oubliez surtout pas de vous abonner à notre chaîne! And for new interesting videos, don't forget to subscribe to our channel!' and 'Merci de votre soutien! À la prochaine! Thank you for your support! See you next time!' The Rocket continues to fly through the dark, starry expanse, with the boys smiling and waving as the scene fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins dynamically with a white and gray Rocket, featuring a blue nose cone and bright yellow flames, speeding through a starry expanse with a distinct whooshing sound, leaving a bright yellow trail before exiting the frame. The CUBE ACADEMY logo is consistently present in the top right. An upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track, with a slightly Latin flavor, then commences and plays continuously as the video transitions to a bright yellow background displaying 'COMMENTS' with five pink stars. A white comment box appears, cycling through viewer feedback in French, Portuguese, and Spanish, praising the video's educational value, storytelling, and pronunciation, with usernames like 'Wendy Hlatshwayo' and 'The Wondering Astronaut' highlighted alongside visible likes, hearts, and reply options. The segment concludes with a soft pink gradient End Card, animated with sparkles and a rotating green ring, displaying 'THANKS FOR WATCHING!', 'WATCH MORE', a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, and the phrase 'DON'T FORGET TO LIKE, COMMENT AND SUBSCRIBE!', all accompanied by the ongoing upbeat music.

entity_library.Binoculars: Green handheld binoculars held by the Boy with Binoculars at the start, which he later puts down as he boards the rocket, no longer visible after the launch.
entity_library.Rooftop Setting: A wooden rooftop with a white railing, overlooking a dark cityscape under a starry night sky, serving as the backdrop for the educational interaction, enhanced by light, whimsical background music.
entity_library.Rocket: A white and gray rocket with a blue nose cone and bright yellow flames, shown moving through space before exiting the frame, serving as the vehicle for the boys' journey, accompanied by a distinct whooshing sound.
entity_library.Mercury: A large, gray, cratered planet depicted in space, shown against a colorful nebula. It is identified as the first planet of the solar system, with on-screen text and graphics highlighting its proximity to the Sun, small size, extreme temperature range (-173°C to 427°C), and 88-day year, all narrated by the Boy in Yellow Shirt.
entity_library.Venus: A large, glowing, rocky planet with a fiery orange and yellow surface, shown in close proximity to the Sun. It is described as Earth’s twin and highlighted for its extreme heat (460°C) and 225-day orbit.
entity_library.Sun: A massive, bright, orange-yellow star with visible solar flares and a glowing corona, dominating the background as the Rocket approaches Venus. It provides intense illumination and a warm, radiant atmosphere for the scene.
entity_library.Earth: A large, blue and white planet with visible continents, oceans, and cloud cover, shown in the distance against a dark, star-filled space. It appears as the next destination, glowing softly as the Rocket approaches and then recedes into the background, and is the subject of the Boy in Yellow Shirt's explanation.
entity_library.Mars: A large, reddish-brown planet with visible surface features, set against a colorful nebula. Its year and temperature range are verbally explained in French, and its nickname 'the Red Planet' is discussed, with its red iron oxide soil composition detailed.
entity_library.Jupiter: A massive gas giant with distinct brown and white bands, visible against a dark, star-filled space. It is introduced as the largest planet in the solar system, with its composition of hydrogen and helium verbally explained in French, emphasizing its size and gaseous nature.
entity_library.Digital Thermometer Graphic: A white, handheld digital thermometer with a blue screen, appearing next to Uranus and later Neptune to visually represent their respective temperature ranges, enhancing the educational content with a clear visual aid.
entity_library.Saturn: A pale yellow gas giant with prominent, thick, multi-layered rings, visible against a dark, star-filled space. It is introduced as the sixth planet from the Sun, the second largest in the solar system, and famous for its beautiful ringlets, with the rings clearly visible and rotating slowly.
entity_library.12 années Sign: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '12 années' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Jupiter’s orbital period.
entity_library.29,5 années Sign: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '29,5 années' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Saturn’s orbital period.
entity_library.Uranus: A large, pale blue planet with a smooth, icy surface, set against a deep blue nebula. It is introduced as an ice giant, with a digital thermometer graphic appearing to show its temperature range of -226°C to -197°C.
entity_library.84 ans Sign: A white, notepad-style sign with red clips, displaying '84 ans' in bold yellow text, floating above the Rocket to visually represent Uranus’s orbital period.
entity_library.Neptune: A large, deep blue planet with subtle cloud bands, set against a vibrant, colorful nebula. It is introduced as an ice giant, with a digital thermometer graphic appearing to show its temperature range of -218°C to -200°C.
entity_library.CUBE ACADEMY Logo: A yellow and blue logo in the top right corner, reading 'CUBE ACADEMY' in bold, stylized text, remaining consistently visible throughout the segment as the channel's branding.
entity_library.Comments Section: A white rectangular box on a yellow background displaying viewer comments in multiple languages, with usernames, timestamps, likes, hearts, and reply options, cycling through different sets of feedback, all set to an upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track.
entity_library.End Card: A soft pink gradient background with animated sparkles and a rotating green ring, featuring bold black text 'THANKS FOR WATCHING!', 'WATCH MORE' on both sides, a red 'SUBSCRIBE' button, and the phrase 'DON'T FORGET TO LIKE, COMMENT AND SUBSCRIBE!' below, accompanied by an upbeat, rhythmic instrumental music track.
